menus.Name: ORZID GMENU ER/UC ACUTE SINUSITIS
menus.Text:
[Help, legend, allergy info, consults, alternative antimicrobials and more \(ER\)](orzid-gmenu-er-uc-abx-general-information-ed)

# ACUTE SINUSITIS

Most cases of acute sinusitis are viral and resolve within 10 days. Symptoms

(nasal congestion, purulent nasal discharge, and tooth or facial pain) can be

treated with decongestants, analgesics, and nasal saline. Diagnosis of acute

sinusitis is based on the presence of at least 2 major or 1 major and at least 2

minor symptoms.

# Major Symptoms

1. Purulent anterior nasal discharge

2. Purulent or discolored posterior nasal discharge

3. Nasal congestion or fullness

4. Facial pain or pressure

5. Hyposmia or anosmia

6. Fever

# Minor Symptoms

1. Headache

2. Ear pain, pressure or fullness

3. Halitosis

4. Dental pain

5. Cough

6. Fatigue

If patient meets criteria above for acute sinusitis, treat with antimicrobials

according to the following criteria for mild to moderate or severe bacterial

sinusitis.

# Mild to moderate bacterial sinusitis

1. Symptoms persisting beyond 10 days

< OR >

2. Double-sickening - Symptoms typical of viral upper respiratory infection

initially improving for 5-6 days then worsening with new development of fever,

headache or increased nasal discharge.

[\[CLICK HERE\] for treatment of mild to moderate bacterial sinusitis - Opt CDSS](orzid3-gmenu-acute-sinusitis-mild-to-mod)

# Severe bacterial sinusitis

Severe signs or symptoms of high fever (>/= 102 degrees Fahrenheit, >/= 39

degrees Celsius) and purulent nasal discharge for 3-4 consecutive days or facial

pain lasting for at least 3-4 consecutive days at the beginning of illness.

[\[CLICK HERE\] for treatment of severe bacterial sinusitis](orzid-gmenu-er-uc-severe-sinusitis)

# _______________________________________________________________________________

References:

Chow et al, Clinical Infectious Diseases. 2012, 54(8) e72-e112

Mandell chapter: Sinusitis

Meltzer et al, J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2004, 114(6) S155-S212.

Rosenfeld et al, Otolaryngology - Head and Neck Surgery. 2015, 152(4) 598-609

menus.Name: ORZID GMENU ER/UC SEVERE SINUSITIS
menus.Text:
[Help, legend, allergy info, consults, alternative antimicrobials and more \(ER\)](orzid-gmenu-er-uc-abx-general-information-ed)

# SEVERE SINUSITIS

Community-acquired bacterial sinusitis is most often due to Streptococcus

pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenza or Moraxella catarrhalis. Group A

Streptococci, Staphylococcus aureus and anaerobes are isolated less frequently.

For patients who present with severe symptoms (high fever (>/= 102 degrees

Fahrenheit, >/= 39 degrees Celsius) and purulent nasal discharge for 3-4

consecutive days or facial pain lasting for at least 3-4 consecutive days at the

beginning of illness), initiate empiric antimicrobials and obtain CT of sinuses

to determine the extent of infection. If the CT shows extension of sinusitis

infection into surrounding structures (i.e. the cranium or orbit) consult ENT

immediately. Closely monitor all patients who present with severe signs and

symptoms to ensure that signs and symptoms improve within 72 hours.

# Outpatient Treatment

Preferred:

[Amoxicillin/clavulanate 875-125 mg PO q12h \($\) \[R\] for 7 days](psozid2-amox-clav-875-125mg-7ds-po-bid)

[Non-VA order for above](pshzid2-amox-clav-875-125mg-7ds-po-bid)

Alternatives:

[Assessment of Beta-lactam Reaction](orzid2-gmenu-abx-beta-lactam-allergy-assesment)

[Cefdinir 300 mg PO q12h \($\) \[R\] for 7 days](psozid2-cefdinir-300mg-po-bid-7days)

[Non-VA order for above](pshzid2-cefdinir-300mg-po-bid-7days)

< OR >

[Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID\($\) \[DI\] for 7 days](psozid2-doxycycline-100mg-po-bid-7ds)

[Non-VA order for above](pshzid2-doxycycline-100mg-po-bid-7ds)

# Treatment of hospitalized patient

Preferred:

[Ampicillin-sulbactam 3gm IV once STAT \($\) \[R\]](psjizid-ampicillin-sulbactam-3gm-iv-once-er-uc)

Alternative:

[Assessment of Beta-lactam Reaction](orzid2-gmenu-abx-beta-lactam-allergy-assesment)

[Ceftriaxone 2 gm IV once STAT \($\) \[M\]](psjizid-ceftriaxone-2gm-iv-once-er-uc)

# Treatment failure

If symptoms worsen or no improvement is seen after 3-5 days of treatment,

broaden coverage, switch to different antimicrobial class and treat 7 days. If

symptoms continue to worsen or there is no improvement after 3-5 more days of

treatment, obtain CT to investigate non-infectious or suppurative causes. ENT

consultation is recommended. If sinuses are aspirated, specimens should be

cultured.

Preferred

[Piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375 gm IV once STAT \($$\) \[R\]](psjizid-pip-tazo-3-375-iv-once-er-uc-ne-extinf-ci-cefep-metro)

< OR >

[Cefepime 2 gm IV once STAT \($$\) \[R\]](psjizid-cefepime-2-gm-iv-once-er-uc)

Alternative:

[Assessment of Beta-lactam Reaction](orzid2-gmenu-abx-beta-lactam-allergy-assesment)

[Meropenem 1 gm IV once STAT \($$\) \[R,O\]](psjizid-meropenem-1gm-iv-once-er-uc)

# _______________________________________________________________________________

References:

Chow et al, Clinical Infectious Diseases. 2012, 54(8) e72-e112

Mandell chapter: Sinusitis

Meltzer et al, J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2004, 114(6) S155-S212.

Rosenfeld et al, Otolaryngology - Head and Neck Surgery. 2015, 152(4) 598-609

menus.Name: ORZID2 GMENU ABX ABOUT THE CDSS
menus.Text:
# ABOUT THE ANTIMICROBIAL COMPUTERIZED DECISION SUPPORT SYSTEM (CDSS)

The Antimicrobial CDSS is a tool within the Computerized Patient Record System

that offers antimicrobial prescribing advice to clinicians at the point of order

entry. The system is structured as an order menu decision tree that contains

quick orders pre-programmed with appropriate antimicrobial regimens for specific

infectious diseases.

# Goals

Improve patient outcomes, reduce rates of microbial resistance, prevent adverse

patient outcomes, optimize costs and improve providers' lives. In addition to

these goals, the program fulfills antimicrobial stewardship quality standards

implemented by the Joint Commission.

# Rationale

*Improved patient outcomes

In a 2013 study, Minneapolis Veterans Affairs Medical Center (MVAMC)

antimicrobial courses ordered through the Antimicrobial CDSS were significantly

more likely to be appropriate from start to completion, (44%) compared to non-

CDSS courses (33%, P = 0.013). This is consistent with other studies that have

shown significant improvement in appropriate use of antimicrobials when CDSS

programs are utilized.

*Restricted formulary

The Minneapolis VAHCS has a restricted antimicrobial formulary. A restricted

formulary reduces cost of health care and is an integral part of antimicrobial

stewardship. The Antimicrobial CDSS provides a method of formulary restriction

that doesn't require a call back from pharmacy or a prior authorization for the

antimicrobial to be approved.

[More information on antimicrobial restriction policy](orzid2-gmenu-abx-abx-restriction-policy)

*Increased efficiency

Along with being able to order restricted antimicrobials without a prior

authorization or call back from pharmacy, the Antimicrobial CDSS provides quick

orders pre-programmed with appropriate antimicrobial regimens for specific

infectious diseases. Using the system saves time and increases the efficiency of

the ordering provider.

# Features

The CDSS has been developed locally with input from a broad, representative

group of clinicians. Recommendations have been based on local susceptibility

patterns, clinical evidence, local preferences, and national guidelines. The

CDSS provides a way for clinicians to select alternative drugs when the

recommended drugs are contraindicated.

Detailed instructions can be found in the link below.

[How to use the CDSS](orzid2-gmenu-abx-how-to-use-cdss)

References

Dellit et al. Clinical Infectious Diseases 2007, 44:159-7

Filice et al. Infection Control and Hospital Epidemiology. 2013, 34.6

Rawson et al. Clinical Microbiology and Infection. 2017, 1e9

menus.Name: ORZID2 GMENU ABX ABX RESTRICTION POLICY
menus.Text:
# ANTIMICROBIAL RESTRICTION POLICY

Medication orders placed using the CDSS system will NOT require Infectious

Disease approval. Because prescribing advice within the system has been

developed with input from a broad, representative group of clinicians and the

recommendations that have emerged are based on site-specific susceptibility

patterns, clinical evidence, local preferences, and national guidelines,

INFECTIOUS DISEASE APPROVAL FOR RESTRICTED ANTIMICROBIALS WILL NOT BE NEEDED at

participating VA sites. Clinicians are still encouraged to seek advice from

Infectious Diseases whenever it would be helpful.

Restricted antimicrobials ordered outside the CDSS system will continue to need

ID approval.

# ADJUSTING EXISTING ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

# To change the DOSE of a CURRENTLY ACTIVE antimicrobial in CPRS

Right click on the active order and change the dose as needed.

# To change from the CURRENT ANTIMICROBIAL MEDICATION

We encourage clinicians to consult the CDSS when considering alternative

antimicrobial therapy due to an adverse drug reaction, a culture result, or a

need to switch from intravenous to oral therapy. Many of the CDSS advice menus

contain more than one antimicrobial recommendation. If a suitable alternative

is not listed on the advice menu, clinicians have the additional resource of an

expanded menu of alternative medications that can be accessed by selecting the

Need an alternative antimicrobial? link at the top of each advice menu. To

order an alternative medication using this link prescribers will need to

indicate the rationale behind the choice.

Note: Restricted antimicrobials ordered outside of the CDSS system will still

require Infectious Diseases approval.

menus.Name: ORZID2 GMENU ABX BETA-LACTAM ALLERGY ASSESMENT
menus.Text:
# ASSESSMENT OF BETA-LACTAM REACTION AND ALTERNATIVE TREATMENT

# Streamlined Version (PDF form)

[Assessment of Beta-lactam Reaction \[CLICK HERE\]](orz-gtx-abx-beta-lactam-reaction-pdf)

This guide is intended to aid clinicians in assessment of beta-lactam

reactions and selection of appropriate alternative therapy. It is not a

substitute for clinical judgment and should not be used as a sole resource when

selecting antimicrobial therapy.

[Important Beta-Lactam Allergy Facts and Obtaining a Detailed Reaction History](orzid2-gmenu-abx-important-beta-lactam-allergy-facts)

# Reaction Assessment and Selection of an Alternative Beta-lactam

- If the same or a different beta-lactam was subsequently tolerated without

reaction, that beta-lactam may be used.

- If reaction was intolerance (e.g. headache, GI symptoms), family history of

reaction, or if the patient denies allergy, any beta-lactam may be used.

- See below for treatment based on type of reaction (severe vs non-severe).

- If reaction is unknown and patient denies any severe reactions (see below for

list of severe reactions), reaction is considered non-severe.

- If patient is in a suitable care setting, observation during the first 15

minutes to 1 hour after first dose of therapy may be performed to monitor for

signs/symptoms of an allergic reaction.

# _______________________________________________________________________________

# SEVERE REACTION TO BETA-LACTAM:

# _______________________________________________________________________________

If the patient experienced a known severe reaction (use link below for severe

reaction list), AVOID ALL Beta-Lactam Antibiotics. If beta-lactam therapy is

ideal, contact Infectious Diseases for desensitization or alternative.

Severe Reaction Examples:

- Anaphylaxis

- Reaction needed medical intervention

- Mucosal, cardiovascular or respiratory system involvement

- Hemolytic anemia

- Immune hepatitis

- Interstitial nephritis

- Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN)

- Skin desquamation

- Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS)

- Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS)

- Generalized exanthematous pustulosis

- Toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN)

# _______________________________________________________________________________

# NON-SEVERE REACTION TO BETA-LACTAM:

# _______________________________________________________________________________

Select an alternative non-cross-reactive beta-lactam for therapy based on

beta-lactam class patient had a reaction to. Refer to PDF document above for

detailed cross-reactivity chart.

# PENICILLIN CLASS REACTION

(e.g. Amoxicillin, ampicillin, cloxacillin, dicloxacillin, nafcillin, oxacillin,

penicillin, piperacillin, ticarcillin)

# Use: Cefazolin, cefdinir, cefuroxime, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, cefepime,

# ertapenem, imipenem, or meropenem

# 1ST GENERATION CEPHALOSPORIN CLASS REACTION

(e.g. Cefadroxil, cefatrizine, cefazolin, ceftezole, cephalexin, cephalothin,

cephapirin)

# Use: Dicloxacillin, nafcillin, cefazolin (unless reaction was to cefazolin)

# cefdinir, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, cefuroxime, cefepime, ertapenem, imipenem,

# or meropenem

# 2ND GENERATION CEPHALOSPORIN CLASS REACTION

(e.g. Cefaclor, cefamandole, cefonicid, cefotetan, cefoxitin, cefprozil,

cefuroxime)

# Use: Amoxicillin, ampicillin, dicloxacillin, nafcillin, piperacillin,

# cefadroxil cefazolin, cefdinir, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, cephalexin, cefepime,

# ertapenem imipenem, or meropenem

# 3RD-5TH GENERATION CEPHALOSPORIN CLASS REACTION

(e.g. Cefdinir, cefditoren, cefepime, cefiderocol,cefixime, cefpirome,

cefoperazone, cefotaxime, cefpodoxime, ceftaroline, ceftazidime, ceftibuten,

ceftizoxime, ceftolozane, ceftriaxone)

# Use: Amoxicillin, ampicillin, dicloxacillin, nafcillin, penicillin,

# piperacillin, cefadroxil, cefazolin, cephalexin, ertapenem, imipenem, or

# meropenem

# CARBAPENEM CLASS REACTION

(e.g. Ertapenem, imipenem and meropenem)

# Use: Amoxicillin, ampicillin, dicloxacillin, nafcillin, penicillin,

# piperacillin, cefadroxil, cefazolin, cephalexin, cefdinir, ceftazidime,

# ceftriaxone, or cefepime

# _______________________________________________________________________________

menus.Name: ORZID2 GMENU ABX DRUG ALLERGIES
menus.Text:
# DRUG ALLERGIES

# Importance of history

The nature and circumstances of a possible previous reaction including the

timing should be considered. In many cases, previous reactions are clearly

not due to drug allergy.

# Types of reactions

*Immediate - within one hour of starting drug administration.

Type I reactions present with urticaria, hypotension, angioedema, bronchospasm,

or anaphylaxis and occur within an hour of exposure. These reactions are

immunoglobulin E (IgE) and mast cell and/or basophil mediated.

*Delayed - After one hour of starting administration (usually after 6 days)

1) Type II reactions manifest as hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia and

neutropenia. They are caused by antibody (usually IgG)- mediated cell

destruction.

2) Type III reactions can manifest as serum sickness, vasculitis or Arthus

reaction, and are caused by IgG:drug immune complex deposition and complement

activation.

3) Type IV reactions typically manifest as contact dermatitis, morbilliform

reactions, severe exfoliative dermatoses (Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic

epidermal necrolysis) and other presentations. They are T-cell mediated.

Repeated exposures lead to reactions that are of the same type.

# Beta-lactam allergies

Approximately 10% of patients report a beta-lactam allergy. Up to 90% of these

patients can tolerate penicillin and have been designated penicillin allergic

unnecessarily. Patients allergic to cephalosporins will have a cross-reaction to

penicillins 3% to 5% of the time. Overall, patients with a history of penicillin

allergy can tolerate cephalosporins although there are rare reports to severe,

sometimes fatal anaphylactic reactions. Neither penicillin- or cephalosporin-

allergic patients will react to aztreonam. The degree of cross-reactivity of

penicillins and cephalosporins with carbapenems appears to be low.

# Therapeutic implications

If a patient has had an immediate reaction to a drug in the past, an allergy

specialist should evaluate the patient before a drug of the same class is

administered to the patient. Patients with delayed onset reactions causing

fever, systemic symptoms or mucosal involvement should also be evaluated by an

allergy specialist before a drug of the same class is administered.

# Desensitization

A patient with previous Type I reaction to a beta-lactam can be desensitized and

then receive therapeutic doses of the same class within a day. Severe

reactions during desensitization are uncommon. Pharmacy service must be

contacted for assistance and drug preparation. Infectious Disease consultation

strongly recommended.

[Link to penicillin desensitization protocol](orzid2-gmenu-abx-penicillin-desens-protocol)

References

JAMA 2001, 285: 249

Joint Task Force on Practice Parameters: Drug Allergy - An Updated Practice

Parameter (2010)

Kelkar PS NEJM 2001, 345: 804

Mayo Clinic Proc 1999, 74: 290

Up-to-Date article: Cephalosporin-allergic patients: Subsequent use of

cephalosporins and related antibiotics

Up-to-Date article: Drug allergy: Classification and clinical features

Weiss ME, Adkinson NF. Immediate hypersensitivity reactions to penicillin and

related antibiotics. Clin Allergy 1988, 18:515

menus.Name: ORZID2 GMENU ABX DURATION OF ABX THERAPY
menus.Text:
# DURATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

In general, duration of therapy is based on multiple factors, including

infection severity, rapidity of improvement, and pathogen susceptibility. For a

few diseases guidelines are established based on available evidence and opinion.

For many more diseases, guidelines have not been established.

For outpatient prescriptions, when a range is recommended by CDSS, the

prescription order will default to the lowest dose and day supply. For inpatient

orders, durations are listed in the recommendations but are not included in the

quick order. The provider must select a duration, if desired, when completing an

inpatient quick order.

For all cases clinical judgment is important. More information is given within

specific diagnoses or syndromes sections.

menus.Name: ORZID2 GMENU ABX ELEMENTS OF CDSS ADVICE PAGE
menus.Term2: Elements of a CDSS advice page
menus.Text:
# ELEMENTS OF A CDSS ADVICE MENU

Advice menus are comprised of the following elements:

# _____________________________________

# TYPICALLY present on an advice menu

# _____________________________________

1) Diagnosis or condition label-at the top of each advice menu.

2) Treatment. Empirical treatment as well as treatment for selected pathogens

is presented with links to antimicrobial quick orders unless stated otherwise.

In addition to the drug, dose, and route, each quick order text includes the

drug's approximate cost per day and coding for important drug properties of

note. Drugs clustered together are considered equivalent.

3) Help. At the top of each advice menu. There are links to a variety of

resources (susceptibilities, important drug properties, section-specific

consults, etc.).

4) References. At the bottom on each advice menu. These are key references for

the recommendations on the menu.

# _____________________________________

# DISCRETIONARY advice menu components

# _____________________________________

1) (Treatment) Alternative. Following the (preferred) Treatment section with

quick orders for common alternative antimicrobials. Antimicrobial listed under

each alternative header are equivalents among those listed. A broader selection

of medications can be accessed through the Need an alternative menu within the

help section. For some conditions we recommend alternative antimicrobials when

recommended drugs are not suitable for an individual patient. Unless otherwise

stated drugs clustered together under Alternatives are considered equivalent.

If none of the recommended or alternative drugs is suitable you can find more

drugs by clicking on the Need an alternative link at the top of the menu.

2) Consults. At the top of the menu, these are consultation requests pertinent

to the clinical problem addressed by the menu. Links to context-specific

consults can be found on advice menus, as appropriate. For example, the herpes

simplex encephalitis menu contains a link to a request for neurology consult.

3) Resources. Pertinent to the clinical problem and addressed by the menu is

provided for example. For some menus, supplemental resources have been

identified (e.g., PORT score calculator for Community Acquired Pneumonia,

diagnostic tools, Epidemiology, etc.) the information is included on the advice

menu.

menus.Text:
# DISEASES AND SYNDROMES NOT COVERED

Our system provides advice for the treatment of most infectious diseases likely

to be encountered in our patient population. Diseases that do not require

antimicrobial therapy are not included. For more information on a syndrome not

described in our system, please contact infectious diseases. If you feel that a

syndrome should be included in our system please contact us.

[Contact VISN23 Antimicrobial CDSS Program Staff](orzid2-gmenu-abx-additional-assistance)

# HOME IV AT THE MINNEAPOLIS VA

Minneapolis VA provides home IV therapy contracts with commercial providers. To

order home IV antimicrobials:

# ___________________________________

# Step 1

# ___________________________________

An Infectious Disease staff physician must approve outpatient IV antimicrobials

in each case. Call x4185, or call the operator on off-tours to contact the ID

staff member on-call. If the ID service has been consulted, you must request

Home IV approval and recommendations from the ID staff on-call.

# ___________________________________

# Step 2

# ___________________________________

Order a Home IV consult (click on quick order below). Note: Allow 24 hours for

home IV service assessment, teaching, etc to occur.

[Home IV consult \(or page 7253\)](gmrctz-home-iv-infusion-coordination)

menus.Name: ORZID2 GMENU ABX HOW TO FIND ALT ABX
menus.Text:
# HOW TO FIND AN ALTERNATIVE ANTIMICROBIAL WITHIN THE CDSS

The CDSS has been developed locally with input from a broad, representative

group of clinicians. All antimicrobial recommendations within the Antimicrobial

CDSS are reviewed by a clinical pharmacist and an Infectious Diseases Medical

Specialist. Recommendations provided are based on local susceptibility patterns,

clinical evidence, local preferences, and national guidelines. In rare cases,

the recommended antimicrobials and listed alternatives may not be preferred for

the patient you are treating (e.g. isolate resistance, allergies, etc.). In

these cases, a broader set of alternative antimicrobials may be ordered through

the Antimicrobial CDSS. Like other antimicrobial orders in the CDSS,

antimicrobials ordered through the Alternative Antimicrobials menu do on require

a prior authorization or ID approval.

To access the Alternative Antimicrobials menu, navigate to the menu containing

antimicrobial recommendations for the infectious disease you are treating.

At the top of the menu, select:

#2 Help legend, allergy info, consults, alternative antimicrobials and more

Then select- Alternative Antimicrobials to CDSS Recommendations

Select the reason an alternative antimicrobial is required then the Alternative

Antimicrobial menu will display.

The Alternative Antimicrobial menu contains antimicrobial regimens that have

been deemed appropriate for use as alternatives to recommended antimicrobials by

the Antimicrobial P&T Subcommittee. If the list does not contain the preferred

antimicrobial, contact or consult Infectious Diseases.

The regimens provided in the Alternative Antimicrobial menu are not tailored for

a specific infectious disease. The dose, frequency, and duration must be changed

based on the infectious disease you are treating as well as for patient specific

factors. The usage of alternative antimicrobials are monitored by the

Antimicrobial CDSS team for appropriateness and to identify infectious diseases

for addition to the system.

menus.Name: ORZID2 GMENU ABX HOW TO USE CDSS
menus.Text:
# HOW TO USE THE CDSS

The CDSS has been designed to offer antimicrobial prescribing advice quickly and

efficiently. Since inpatient and outpatient drugs are ordered differently, the

CDSS has both and inpatient and outpatient versions for prescribing.

The Antimicrobial CDSS is located within CPRS. When CPRS is open, click on the

Orders tab at the bottom left hand side of the CPRS window.

# For inpatients

On the left-hand side of the "Orders" tab, click on the "Medicine Inpt Orders."

option.  In the right-hand column of the window that pops up, select "#77

Antimicrobials (CDSS).". The main menu of the Inpatient Antimicrobial CDSS will

now be open.

# For outpatients

On the left-hand side of the "Orders" tab, click on the "Medicine Outpt Orders."

option.In the right-hand column of the window that pops up, select "#55

Antimicrobial (CDSS).". The main menu of the Outpatient Antimicrobial CDSS will

now be open.

# Navigating Within the CDSS

*Using the decision tree

The main menu of the Antimicrobial CDSS contains links to categories of

infectious diseases based on syndrome/organ system (e.g. cardiovascular,

genitourinary, etc.), organisms (e.g. gram-positive bacteria, gram-negative

bacteria, viruses, etc.) and other special cases. To navigate to specific

infectious disease, select the category the infectious disease is associated

with. Within the category menu, select the specific infectious disease sub-menu

for recommendations, quick orders and pertinent resources (e.g., section-

specific consults, important drug properties, CDSS system help resources, etc.).

*Using the Index

The Index is in the main menu at the top of the left-hand column under the

header Index of Antimicrobial CDSS content. The Index contains an alphabetized

list of Antimicrobial CDSS content. Find the infectious disease, organism or

other topic you are looking for by scrolling down the list. When you locate the

term you are looking for, click on it to be linked to the menu containing

specific recommendations, quick orders and pertinent resources.

# Quick Orders

Quick orders are preprogrammed with the usually recommended antimicrobial dose,

frequency and solution for the specific infectious disease.

[More information on duration of antimicrobial therapy](orzid2-gmenu-abx-duration-of-abx-therapy)

Quick orders are not tailored to specific patient factors such as renal

insufficiency, comorbidities, contraindications, weight, allergies, etc. The

provider must assess every patient closely for these factors and modify

recommended antimicrobials if needed. To assist the provider, additional drug

information is provided within specific infectious diseases recommendation menus

and a link to more in depth information is provided at the top of these menus.

Alternatives to preferred antimicrobials quick orders are often provided in

recommendations, a broader selection of alternative antimicrobials is provided

in the Alternative Antimicrobial menu (see What if a Preferred Antimicrobial is

not Provided in the Recommendations?).

# Elements of a CDSS Advice Page

menus.Name: ORZID2 GMENU ABX IMPORTANT BETA-LACTAM ALLERGY FACTS
menus.Text:
# Important Beta-lactam Allergy Facts

1. 10% of patients report an allergy to penicillin.

2. Less than 1% of patients are truly allergic to penicillin.

3. 80% of patients who had an immediate allergic reaction (Type I,

Immunoglobulin E (IgE)-mediated) to penicillin lose sensitivity after 10

years.

4. Cross-reactivity between penicillin and cephalosporins occurs in only 2% of

patients and most patients do not have clinically significant reactions.

# Obtaining a Detailed Reaction History

- Locate documented reaction information within the Computerized Patient Record

System (CPRS)

> Reported reactions can be found in the Cover Sheet under Allergies/Adverse

Reactions. Double click on the specific reaction for more information,

including signs/symptoms and date of reaction.

> A reaction report can also be found here: Reports tab -> Clinical Reports -

Allergies.

- Determine beta-lactam medication history. A list of all locally prescribed

medications can be found here: Reports tab -> Clinical Reports -> Pharmacy ->

All Medications.

- Interview the patient or family for details about the reaction. Questions to

ask during the interview:

> What medication were you taking when the reaction occurred? DO NOT accept

Penicillin or Cephalosporin as an allergy, ensure specific offending

medication is identified.

> How long after taking the medication did the reaction occur? Immediate

reactions typically occur within 1 hour and up to 72 hours after

administration. Delayed reactions occur after 72 hours.

> How long ago did the reaction occur? Did it occur over 10 years ago?

menus.Name: ORZID2 GMENU ABX PENICILLIN DESENS PROTOCOL
menus.Text:
# PENICILLIN DESENSITIZATION PROTOCOL

Pharmacy Service must be involved. Contact satellite pharmacist at ext. 2040 or

menu 648-6746. Infectious Diseases Consult strongly recommended.

When penicillin is the drug of choice for an infectious disease in a patient

with Type I penicillin allergy, one option is to desensitize the patient.

Severe reactions cannot be predicted accurately with skin testing in the

clinical setting.

# Penicillin Desensitization

> Perform in an ICU or similar setting.

> Discontinue all beta-adrenergic antagonists.

> Have IV line in place and EKG and spirometer ready.

> Once desensitization has been accomplished, therapy with this drug must not

lapse or the risk of allergic reactions increases.

> A history of Stevens Johnson syndrome, exfoliative dermatitis, or erythroderma

are nearly absolute contraindications to desensitization.

# _____________________________________

# ORAL ROUTE

# _____________________________________

Oral route is preferred if oral preparation is available and GI tract is

functional. 1/3 of patients will develop transient reaction, usually mild.

# [Step, 15 minute intervals] concentration, volume

[1] 0.5mg/ml, 0.1ml

[2] 0.5mg/ml, 0.2ml

[3] 0.5mg/ml, 0.4ml

[4] 0.5mg/ml, 0.8ml

[5] 0.5mg/ml, 1.6ml

[6] 0.5mg/ml, 3.2ml

[7] 0.5mg/ml, 6.4ml

[8] 5mg/ml, 1.2ml

[9] 5mg/ml, 2.4ml

[10] 5mg/ml, 4.8ml

[11] 50mg/ml, 1ml

[12] 50mg/ml, 2ml

[13] 50mg/ml, 4ml

[14] 50mg/ml, 8ml

After step 14, observe 30 minutes. If no reaction, give 1 gm IV.

# _____________________________________

# PARENTERAL ROUTE

# _____________________________________

# [Step, 15 minute intervals] concentration, volume

[1] 0.1mg/ml, 0.1ml

[2] 0.1mg/ml, 0.2ml

[3] 0.1mg/ml, 0.4ml

[4] 0.1mg/ml, 0.8ml

[5] 1mg/ml, 0.16ml

[6] 1mg/ml, 0.32ml

[7] 1mg/ml, 0.64ml

[8] 10mg/ml, 0.12ml

[9] 10mg/ml, 0.24ml

[11] 100mg/ml, 0.1ml

[12] 100mg/ml, 0.2ml

[13] 100mg/ml, 0.4ml

[14] 100mg/ml, 0.8ml

[15] 1000mg/ml, 0.16ml

[16] 1000mg/ml, 0.32ml

[17] 1000mg/ml, 0.64ml

After step 17, observe 30 minutes. If no reaction, give 1 gm IV.

menus.Name: ORZID2 GMENU ACUTE SINUSITIS
menus.Text:
[Help, legend, allergy info, consults, alternative antimicrobials and more \(Inpt\)](orzid2-gmenu-abx-general-information-inpatient)

# ACUTE SINUSITIS

Most cases of acute sinusitis are viral and resolve within 10 days. Symptoms

(nasal congestion, purulent nasal discharge, and tooth or facial pain) can be

treated with decongestants, analgesics, and nasal saline. Diagnosis of acute

sinusitis is based on the presence of at least 2 major or 1 major and at least 2

minor symptoms.

# Major Symptoms

1. Purulent anterior nasal discharge

2. Purulent or discolored posterior nasal discharge

3. Nasal congestion or fullness

4. Facial pain or pressure

5. Hyposmia or anosmia

6. Fever

# Minor Symptoms

1. Headache

2. Ear pain, pressure or fullness

3. Halitosis

4. Dental pain

5. Cough

6. Fatigue

If patient meets criteria above for acute sinusitis, treat with antimicrobials

according to the following criteria for mild to moderate or severe bacterial

sinusitis.

# Mild to moderate bacterial sinusitis

1. Symptoms persisting beyond 10 days

< OR >

2. Double-sickening - Symptoms typical of viral upper respiratory infection

initially improving for 5-6 days then worsening with new development of fever,

headache or increased nasal discharge.

[\[CLICK HERE\] for treatment of mild to moderate bacterial sinusitis](orzid2-gmenu-mild-to-mod-sinusitis)

# Severe bacterial sinusitis

Severe signs or symptoms of high fever (>/= 102 degrees Fahrenheit, >/= 39

degrees Celsius) and purulent nasal discharge for 3-4 consecutive days or facial

pain lasting for at least 3-4 consecutive days at the beginning of illness.

[\[CLICK HERE\] for treatment of severe bacterial sinusitis](orzid2-gmenu-severe-sinusitis)

# _______________________________________________________________________________

References:

Chow et al, Clinical Infectious Diseases. 2012, 54(8) e72-e112

Mandell chapter: Sinusitis

Meltzer et al, J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2004, 114(6) S155-S212.

Rosenfeld et al, Otolaryngology - Head and Neck Surgery. 2015, 152(4) 598-609

manifestations and diagnosis

menus.Text:
[Help, legend, allergy info, consults, alternative antimicrobials and more \(Opt\)](orzid3-gmenu-abx-general-information-outpatient)

# MILD TO MODERATE SINUSITIS

Community-acquired bacterial sinusitis is most often due to Streptococcus

pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae or Moraxella catarrhalis. Streptococci,

Staphylococcus aureus and anaerobes are isolated less frequently.

# Treatment for first 10 days of illness

Treat with decongestants, analgesics, and nasal saline spray.

[\[CLICK HERE\] to go to acute respiratory infection symptomatic treatment menu](orzid3-gmenu-ari-symptomatic-therapy)

# _______________________________________________________________________________

# Treatment of cases that have not improved in 10 days despite

# treatment with decongestants, analgesics, and nasal saline.

Preferred:

[Amoxicillin/clavulanate 875-125 mg PO q12h \($\) \[R\] for 5 days](psozid2-amox-clav-875-125mg-5ds-po-bid)

[Non-VA order for above](pshzid2-amox-clav-875-125mg-5ds-po-bid)

Alternatives:

[Assessment of Beta-lactam Reaction](orzid2-gmenu-abx-beta-lactam-allergy-assesment)

[Cefdinir 300 mg PO q12h \($\) \[R\] for 5 days](psozid2-cefdinir-300mg-po-bid-5days)

[Non-VA order for above](pshzid2-cefdinir-300mg-po-bid-5days)

< OR >

[Doxycycline 100 mg PO q12h \($\) \[DI\] for 5 days](psozid2-doxycycline-100mg-po-bid-5d)

[Non-VA order for above](pshzid2-doxycycline-100mg-po-bid-5d)

# Treatment Failure

If symptoms worsen despite 72 hours or  fail to improve after 3-5 days of

initial empirical antimicrobial therapy, broaden coverage, switch to different

antimicrobial class and treat 7-10 days. If symptoms continue to worsen or there

is no improvement after 3-5 more days of treatment, obtain CT to investigate

non-infectious or suppurative causes. ENT consultation is recommended. If

sinuses are aspirated, specimens should be cultured.

[Levofloxacin 750 mg PO q24h \($\) \[R,DI\] for 7 days](psozid2-levofloxacin-7ds-750mg-po-qday)

[Non-VA order for above](pshzid2-levofloxacin-7ds-750mg-po-qday)

menus.Name: ORZID3 GMENU ACUTE SINUSITIS SEVERE
menus.Text:
[Help, legend, allergy info, consults, alternative antimicrobials and more \(Opt\)](orzid3-gmenu-abx-general-information-outpatient)

# SEVERE SINUSITIS

Community-acquired bacterial sinusitis is most often due to Streptococcus

pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenza or Moraxella catarrhalis. Group A

Streptococci, Staphylococcus aureus and anaerobes are isolated less frequently.

For patients who present with severe symptoms (high fever (>/= 102 degrees

Fahrenheit, >/= 39 degrees Celsius) and purulent nasal discharge for 3-4

consecutive days or facial pain lasting for at least 3-4 consecutive days at the

beginning of illness), initiate empiric antimicrobials and obtain CT of sinuses

to determine the extent of infection. If the CT shows extension of sinusitis

infection into surrounding structures (i.e. the cranium or orbit) consult ENT

immediately. Closely monitor all patients who present with severe signs and

symptoms to ensure that signs and symptoms improve within 72 hours.

# Outpatient Treatment

Preferred:

[Amoxicillin/clavulanate 875-125 mg PO q12h \($\) \[R\] for 7 days](psozid2-amox-clav-875-125mg-7ds-po-bid)

[Non-VA order for above](pshzid2-amox-clav-875-125mg-7ds-po-bid)

Alternatives:

[Assessment of Beta-lactam Reaction](orzid2-gmenu-abx-beta-lactam-allergy-assesment)

[Cefdinir 300 mg PO q12h \($\) \[R\] for 7 days](psozid2-cefdinir-300mg-po-bid-7days)

[Non-VA order for above](pshzid2-cefdinir-300mg-po-bid-7days)

< OR >

[Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID\($\) \[DI\] for 7 days](psozid2-doxycycline-100mg-po-bid-7ds)

[Non-VA order for above](pshzid2-doxycycline-100mg-po-bid-7ds)

# Treatment of hospitalized patient

Refer to the Inpatient Antimicrobial CDSS for parenteral therapy

# Treatment failure

If symptoms worsen or no improvement is seen after 3-5 days of treatment,

broaden coverage, switch to different antimicrobial class and treat 10 days. If

symptoms continue to worsen or there is no improvement after 3-5 more days of

treatment, obtain CT to investigate non-infectious or suppurative causes. ENT

consultation is recommended. If sinuses are aspirated, specimens should be

cultured.

Refer to the Inpatient Antimicrobial CDSS for parenteral therapy

# _______________________________________________________________________________

menus.Name: ORZID3 GMENU ARI SYMPTOMATIC THERAPY
menus.Text:
# Acute Respiratory Infection (ARI) Symptomatic Therapy

[Contact the CDSS Team](orzid2-gmenu-abx-additional-assistance)

# Per CDC: Antibiotics Aren't Always the Answer. Be Antibiotics Aware.

Antibiotics do not work on viruses, such as colds and flu, or runny noses,

even if the mucus is thick, yellow or green. Antibiotics also won't help

some common bacterial infections including most cases of bronchitis, many

sinus infections, and some ear infections. When antibiotics aren't needed,

they won't help you, and the side effects could still hurt you. Reactions

from antibiotics cause 1 out of 5 medication-related visits to ED.

# Select links below to print educational material for patient:

[<Link> CDC Print Pamphlet-Symptomatic RX for Acute Respiratory Infections](orz-gtx-abx-url-rx-prescription-for-viruses)

[<Link> CDC Print Pamphlet-Antibiotics aren't always the answer](orz-gtx-abx-url-cdc-antibiotics-aren-t-the-answer)

# ___________________________________________________________________

# ARI: Symptom-based therapy represents the mainstay of treatment.

Please select only one item from each symptom category patient experencing.

# Antitussive Therapy:

[Guaifenesin Dextromethorphan 10 ML PO q4h prn](psozid-guaifenesin-dm-10ml-q4h-prn-cough)

menus.Name: ORZID3 GMENU PREGNANCY RISK INFO
menus.Term2: Risk factors for antimicrobials in pregnancy
menus.Text:
# RISK FACTORS FOR ANTIMICROBIALS DURING PREGNANCY

# The FDA amended its rules in 2015 concerning the Pregnancy, Labor and delivery,

# and Nursing mothers subsections of the Use in Specific Populations section of

# product labeling. Pregnancy categories A, B, C, D, and X have been removed.

# Drugs approved after June 30 will use the new format immediately, while drugs

# approved after June 30, 2001 will be phased in gradually.

See Sanford Guide for comprehensive antimicrobial pregnancy risk and lactation

safety information

[Sanford Guide Pregnancy Risk & Lactation Safety Table](orz-gtx-abx-sanford-pregnancy)

Risk Factors (A,B,C,D,X) were assigned to all drugs, based on the

level of risk the drug poses to the fetus. Risk Factors were designed to

help the clinician quickly classify a drug for use during pregnancy. They

do not refer to breastfeeding risk. The definitions of the Risk Factors

are those used by the FDA.

# Category Description

# A Controlled studies show no risk

Adequate, well-controlled studies in pregnant women

have failed to demonstrate risk to the fetus.

# B No evidence of risk in humans

Either animal findings show risk (but human

findings do not) or, if no adequate human studies

have been done, animal findings are negative.

# C Risk cannot be ruled out

Human studies are lacking and animal studies

are either positive for fetal risk or lacking

as well. However, potential benefits may

justify the potential risk.

# D Positive evidence of risk

Investigational or postmarketing data show risk

to fetus. Nevertheless, potential benefits may

outweigh the risk.

# X Contraindicated in pregnancy

Studies in animals or humans, or investigational

or postmarketing reports have shown fetal risk

which clearly outweighs any possible benefit to

the patient.

References

Up-to-Date website

Sanford website

menus.Name: ORZID3 GMENU WARFARIN DRUG INTERACTIONS
menus.Text:
# DRUG-DRUG INTERACTIONS BETWEEN ANTIMICROBIALS AND WARFARIN

Several antimicrobials will affect metabolism or the effect of

warfarin on a patient's clotting function. If possible, avoid using an

antimicrobial with a significant interaction with warfarin. If you

decide that an antimicrobial that interacts with warfarin is needed or

you stop an antimicrobial that could interact with warfarin, you

should determine the INR more frequently than usual and adjust dosage

accordingly. Instruct patients to inform their primary warfarin

provider whenever an antimicrobial is prescribed for them.

Antimicrobials can alter warfarin's anticoagulant effect by decreasing

intestinal synthesis or absorption of vitamin K or by affecting

distribution or metabolism of the vitamin. Some antimicrobials increase

synthesis of coagulation factors.

Antimicrobials can decrease the rate of warfarin absorption, compete

with warfarin at sites of warfarin metabolism, induce warfarin

metabolic enzymes, or inhibit the function or synthesis warfarin

metabolic enzymes. Some antimicrobials displace warfarin from protein

binding sites.

Check for warfarin-antimicrobial interactions on Lexicomp:

[Lexicomp Drug Interactions](orz-gtx-abx-lexicomp-interactions)

References

Holbrook, Anne M., et al. Archives of internal medicine 165.10 (2005): 1095-

1106

Lexicomp website
